content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)'. Hungary cannot feasibly implement a structural reform eliminating or significantly delaying early selection before 2030 without (a) substantial prior teacher-workforce stabilization in rural and STEM areas, (b) explicit sequencing away from concurrent National Core Curriculum and AI-integration reforms, and (c) multi-party legislative consensus to protect against political reversal—only the first precondition is currently in progress.
opportunities: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.
strengths: Hungary's teaching workforce is structurally aging and contracting: approximately 50% of full-time primary teachers are age 50 or older, only 7.2% are under 30, and total teacher numbers have declined by over 21,000 since 2003/2004 (164,000 in 2023/2024 vs. 185,000+ in 2003/2004).

Teacher shortages in mathematics and natural sciences are acute; fewer than 3,400 people applied for university pedagogical degrees in 2024, with history–English combinations dominating demand for other subjects.
threats: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.
weaknesses: The actual magnitude of net positive equity gain in Hungary from delayed selection (holding school-choice and catchment rules constant) remains unknown; Polish gains may not generalize given Hungary's significantly higher baseline segregation and denser network of small rural schools.

The cost (in transition staffing, retraining, and administrative disruption) of a full or partial phase-out of early selection in Hungary is unquantified; Polish and other OECD countries' transition budgets are not published in comparable form.
